title: Ideal Beauty — 阅读策略训练
subject: 英语
description: 围绕 Ideal Beauty 课文，通过预测、略读、精读、评价、总结五步阅读法训练英语阅读策略。
teachingNotes: 高一英语阅读策略训练课，围绕 B7U2 Ideal Beauty 课文，通过 5 步阅读法（Predict → Skim → Scan+Matrix → Evaluate → Recap）引导学生从信息提取到批判性思维。三端联动教学：教师控制台推进课堂节奏，学生端完成任务，投屏黑板展示结构化板书。
lessonIntro:
Hello! Welcome to today's English reading lesson.

Today we're reading **Ideal Beauty**. Keep one big question in mind:

*Is one idea of physical beauty really more attractive than another?*

We'll complete 5 tasks. Let's begin!
lessonSummary:
Great job today!

You explored whether one beauty standard is better than another, and practiced 4 reading strategies.

Reading is not just understanding words — it's organizing evidence and forming your own judgment.

Homework: "Beyond the Plate" using today's 4 steps.

paragraphs.text: Nigerian teenager Happiness Edem had just one aim in life: to put on weight. So she spent six months in a "fattening room" where her daily routine was to sleep, eat and grow fat. She went in weighing 60 kg, but came out weighing twice that. In some parts of Africa, being fat is desirable because it is a sign of attractiveness in women and power and wealth in men.

However, in magazines and in the media we are bombarded with images of slim women with a fair complexion and handsome, broad-shouldered young men. It is fairly rare to see short-sighted, middle-aged models. Some people question these shallow beauty ideals. Is one idea of physical beauty really more attractive than another?

Ideas about physical beauty change over time and different periods of history reveal different views of beauty, particularly of women. Egyptian paintings often show slim dark-haired women as the normal practice, while one of the earliest representations of women in art in Europe is an overweight female. This is the Venus of Hohle Fels and it is more than 35,000 years old.

In the early 1600s, artists like Peter Paul Rubens also painted plump, pale-skinned women who were thought to be the most stunning examples of female beauty at that time. In Elizabethan England, pale skin was still fashionable because it was a sign of wealth: the make-up used to achieve this look was expensive, so only rich people could afford it.

Within different cultures around the world, there is a huge difference in what is considered beautiful. Traditional customs, like tattooing, head-shaving, piercing or other kinds of bodily changes can express social position, identity or values.

In Borneo, for instance, tattoos are like a diary because they are a written record of all the important events and places a man has experienced in his life. For New Zealand's Maoris they reflect the person's position in society. Western society used to have a very low opinion of tattoos. Today they are considered a popular form of body art among the new generation.

For Europeans, the tradition of using metal rings to stretch a girl's neck may be shocking, but the Myanmar people consider women with long and thin necks more elegant. In Indonesia, the custom of sharpening girls' teeth to points might seem very odd while it is perfectly acceptable in other places to straighten children's teeth with braces. Wearing rings in the nose or plastic surgery might be seen as ugly and unattractive by some cultures, but it is commonplace in many others.

It appears that through the ages and across different cultures, people have always changed their bodies and faces for a wide variety of reasons: sometimes to help them look more beautiful, and sometimes to enable them to show social position or display group identity. Whether it is wearing make-up or decorating the body with tattoos, rings and piercings, different cultures view these things with different eyes. Does this mean that we are all beautiful in our own way?

aiReferenceQA.a: Skimming 是一种快速阅读策略，指通过浏览标题、首句和关键词来获取文章大意，而不需要逐字阅读。
信号词（signal words）帮助你判断段落的功能和结构。比如 'however' 表示转折，'for example' 表示举例，它们是理解文章逻辑的路标。
根据课文 ¶1，在非洲部分地区，肥胖被视为女性的吸引力和男性的权力与财富的象征。文中提到 'being fat is desirable because it is a sign of attractiveness in women and power and wealth in men.'
想想看：文章从 ¶1 开始引出话题，然后每个段落讲一个不同国家的例子。你能找到哪些段落在讲不同国家吗？试着把它们分组。
Evaluating 是批判性思考策略。读完一段后，问自己：我同意作者的观点吗？作者的证据充分吗？这和我知道的是否一致？

readingSteps.description: 课前导读：了解本节课的学习目标和阅读方法。

studentView.title: Welcome to Reading Strategies
studentView.body:
Today we're reading an article about beauty standards around the world — Ideal Beauty.

You'll learn 4 reading strategies that help you read any expository or argumentative text quickly and effectively:

  1. Predicting — read the title, activate what you already know
  2. Skimming — read first sentences only, find the skeleton
  3. Scanning — read with a purpose, extract key details
  4. Evaluating — think critically using evidence

Ready? Let's get started!
keyPoints: This lesson has 5 tasks, each with a strategy briefing beforehand
You don't need to read every word — strategies help you get more from less
Don't worry about new vocabulary — focus on signal words and structure
studentView.ttsText: Welcome to Reading Strategies. Today we're reading an article about beauty standards around the world — Ideal Beauty. You'll learn 4 reading strategies that help you read any expository or argumentative text quickly and effectively. First, Predicting — read the title, activate what you already know. Second, Skimming — read first sentences only, find the skeleton. Third, Scanning — read with a purpose, extract key details. And fourth, Evaluating — think critically using evidence. Ready? Let's get started! Remember: this lesson has 5 tasks, each with a strategy briefing beforehand. You don't need to read every word — strategies help you get more from less. Don't worry about new vocabulary — focus on signal words and structure.

teacherView.speechLine: 学生正在阅读课前导读

cueCards.title: 导读说明
cueCards.body:
学生正在自主阅读课前导读，了解本节课的四种阅读策略。
无需干预，等待学生点击「我准备好了」后自动进入 Step 1。

readingSteps.description: 引导学生关注标题 Ideal Beauty，快速提取 ¶1-2 的核心冲突：尼日利亚增肥室 vs 现代媒体审美。
readingSteps.subtitle: What is beauty?
readingSteps.summary:
You found the central conflict: one culture values gaining weight, while modern media promotes being slim.

Key question: *Is one idea of physical beauty really more attractive than another?*

Let's see how the writer answers it.

answers.questionText: What did Happiness Edem do to become "beautiful"?
answers.questionTranslate: Happiness Edem 为了变"美"做了什么？
options: Went on a diet to become slim
Gained weight in a fattening room
Got cosmetic surgery
Started a fashion brand
answers.hint: Look at ¶1: what happened to her **weight**? Did it go up or down?
answers.hintZh: 看 ¶1，她的**体重**发生了什么变化？增加还是减少？
answers.walkthrough: Read the 2nd sentence of ¶1: *'She spent six months in a **fattening room** where her daily routine was to sleep, eat and **grow fat**.'* The key phrase is 'fattening room' + 'grow fat' — she deliberately gained weight. Which option matches? → B
answers.walkthroughZh: 读 ¶1 第 2 句：*'She spent six months in a **fattening room**…**grow fat**.'* 关键词是 'fattening room' + 'grow fat'——她刻意增重。哪个选项匹配？→ B

answers.questionText: What kind of beauty does modern media promote?
options: Plump and strong
Diverse and inclusive
Slim and fair-skinned
Tattooed and unique
answers.hint: Look at ¶2: "**in the media** we are bombarded with images of..." — what kind of images?
answers.hintZh: 看 ¶2：**媒体**给我们展示了什么样的 images？找 "**slim**" 和 "**fair**"。
answers.walkthrough: ¶2 says: *'in the media we are bombarded with images of **slim** women with a **fair complexion**.'* Two keywords: **slim** + **fair**. Now check the options — only C says 'Slim and fair-skinned'. → C
answers.walkthroughZh: ¶2 说：*'in the media we are bombarded with images of **slim** women with a **fair complexion**.'* 两个关键词：**slim** + **fair**。对照选项——只有 C 说 'Slim and fair-skinned'。→ C

answers.questionText: What is the writer's main question?
answers.questionTranslate: 作者真正想问的大问题是什么？
options: Why do people want to be beautiful?
Is one idea of physical beauty really more attractive than another?
How can we become more beautiful?
Why is the media so powerful?
answers.hint: Read the **last sentence** of ¶2 — the writer is asking a **question**. What is it?
answers.hintZh: 仔细读 ¶2 的**最后一句**——作者在问什么**问题**？
answers.walkthrough: The very last sentence of ¶2 is: *'**Is one idea of physical beauty really more attractive than another?**'* This is a direct question — the writer's central argument revolves around it. Which option matches word-for-word? → B
answers.walkthroughZh: ¶2 最后一句：*'**Is one idea of physical beauty really more attractive than another?**'* 这是作者的核心问题——整篇文章围绕它展开。哪个选项一字不差？→ B

discuss.goal: Student should recognize that the text opens with a deliberate conflict between two opposing beauty standards, and that the writer's core question is whether any single standard of beauty is truly superior.
discuss.openingQ: You've just read about Happiness Edem and modern media. I'm curious — why do you think the writer chose to start the text with these two very different examples? What effect does that create?
discuss.openingQZh: 你觉得作者为什么选择用这两个截然不同的例子开头？这样写有什么效果？

clusters.label: 表面对比理解
clusters.description: 学生只看到两个例子的表面差异（胖vs瘦），没有识别到写作意图。示例：'因为一个地方觉得胖好看一个觉得瘦好看' / '这两个例子说明不同文化审美不同'

clusters.label: 读者效果感知
clusters.description: 学生感知到对比开头引发了好奇或疑问，但说不出 conflict opening 这个策略。示例：'这样开头让人想知道为什么' / '读者会想到底哪个对'

clusters.label: 写作策略识别
clusters.description: 学生明确识别到作者用两个对立观点开头是写作策略（conflict opening），用来制造冲突、引出核心问题。示例：'作者故意放两个相反的例子来引出问题' / 'this is a conflict opening strategy'

targetPoints.label: 识别冲突开头
targetPoints.description: 学生提到两种审美标准之间存在对立/冲突/对比。示例：'一个说胖好看一个说瘦好看，两个完全相反' / 'the writer puts two opposite ideas together'

targetPoints.label: 核心问题意识
targetPoints.description: 学生意识到作者通过对比引出核心问题：是否有唯一正确的审美标准。示例：'作者想让读者想哪种审美才是对的' / 'the question is whether one beauty standard is better'

discuss.systemPrompt:
You are a Socratic English reading tutor for a Chinese high school student reading "Ideal Beauty."

LEARNING GOAL: The student should notice that the writer opens with two contrasting examples of beauty (Nigerian fattening rooms vs. slim media models) ON PURPOSE — to make readers think or question. The student does NOT need to use the term "conflict opening." Recognizing the contrast is intentional and thought-provoking is enough.

RULES:
- NEVER state the answer directly. Guide through questions.
- Ask ONE focused question per turn. Keep it short (2-3 sentences max).
- If the student says something partially correct, acknowledge it warmly, then probe deeper.
- If the student is stuck, narrow the question or offer a small clue (e.g., "Look at how ¶1 and ¶2 contrast...").
- Use simple English. The student is a Chinese high school learner.
- If the student writes in Chinese, respond in English but show you understood their Chinese.
- If the student demonstrates full understanding of the goal, respond with exactly: [GOAL_REACHED] followed by a brief congratulatory summary.
- Be warm, encouraging, never condescending.
- Do NOT number your questions. Be conversational.
- IMPORTANT: Give [GOAL_REACHED] when the student shows meaningful partial understanding — they don't need perfect or complete articulation. If they grasp the core idea even roughly, that counts. Err on the side of encouraging the student rather than blocking them.

fallbackMC.question: Why does the writer start the text with the story of Happiness Edem and then mention modern media beauty standards?
fallbackMC.questionZh: 作者为什么先讲 Happiness Edem 的故事，然后提到现代媒体的审美标准？
options: To show that Nigerian culture is better than Western culture
To create a conflict that makes readers question whether one beauty standard is better than another
To explain why people should gain weight
To advertise fashion magazines
fallbackMC.explanation: The writer deliberately opens with two *opposing* beauty standards — gaining weight in Nigeria vs. being slim in modern media. This conflict is a classic argumentative technique: present two contradicting facts, then ask a big question. Here the question is: "Is one idea of physical beauty really more attractive than another?" This sets up the entire text.
fallbackMC.explanationZh: 作者故意用两种对立的审美标准开头——尼日利亚的增肥 vs 现代媒体的纤瘦。这是经典议论文手法：呈现矛盾事实，再提出核心问题。

discuss.insight: The text uses a "conflict opening" — two opposing facts, then a question. This is common in argumentative writing.
discuss.insightZh: 文章用"冲突开头"——先给两个对立事实，再提出问题。这是议论文常见写法。

teacherView.speechLine: Look at the title — **Ideal Beauty**. Before reading, what comes to mind?
teacherView.focusSubtitle: ¶1-2 · 尼日利亚 vs 现代媒体

quickActions.label: 标题联想
quickActions.message: 看标题 Ideal Beauty，你脑海里想到什么？试着说出 3 个关键词。

quickActions.label: 读¶1尼日利亚
quickActions.message: 请阅读第1段：在尼日利亚，结婚前要去 fattening rooms — 胖=富有。这和你的认知一样吗？

quickActions.label: 对比提示
quickActions.message: ¶1 说胖是美，¶2 说 slim and fair 才是美 — 谁对？带着这个问题继续读。

quickActions.label: 再给 2 分钟
quickActions.message: 再给大家 2 分钟时间完成当前任务

cueCards.title: 激活策略
cueCards.body:
不要急着读全文。先让学生看标题 + ¶1，形成「预期」。

提问模板：
• "What do you think 'ideal beauty' means?"
• "Is there only ONE definition?"

cueCards.title: ¶1-2 要点
cueCards.body:
¶1: Nigeria — fattening rooms — 60→120kg — fat = wealth
¶2: fashion magazines — slim & fair — shallow beauty ideals

核心冲突：文化多样性 vs 媒体单一审美

cueCards.title: 过渡到 Step 2
cueCards.body:
当多数学生能说出「不同文化有不同审美」时，引导过渡：
"Good. Now let's see HOW the author organizes these ideas. Read only the first sentence of each paragraph."

studentView.title: What is Predicting?
studentView.body:
Predicting means using clues — like the title, images, and first paragraph — to guess what a text is about before you read it fully.

Why does this work? When you predict, your brain activates prior knowledge — everything you already know about the topic. This makes new information easier to understand and remember.

Try this now:

  1. Read the title: Ideal Beauty — what does "ideal" mean here?
  2. Skim ¶1 quickly — what specific example does the writer give?
  3. Skim ¶2 — does it agree or disagree with ¶1?

Then answer the 3 questions to check your predictions.
keyPoints: Predicting = title + first glance → activate what you already know
Look for a CONFLICT: two opposing ideas about beauty
Don't read every word yet — just enough to form a prediction
studentView.confirmLabel: I've read the title — let's predict
studentView.ttsText: What is Predicting? Predicting means using clues — like the title, images, and first paragraph — to guess what a text is about before you read it fully. Why does this work? When you predict, your brain activates prior knowledge — everything you already know about the topic. This makes new information easier to understand and remember. Try this now: Read the title, Ideal Beauty — what does "ideal" mean here? Skim paragraph 1 quickly — what specific example does the writer give? Skim paragraph 2 — does it agree or disagree with paragraph 1? Then answer the 3 questions to check your predictions. Remember: predicting equals title plus first glance — activate what you already know. Look for a conflict: two opposing ideas about beauty. Don't read every word yet — just enough to form a prediction.

readingSteps.description: 只读 ¶3-8 首句 + 关键转折词，提炼文章骨架：History → Culture → Conclusion。

aiHints.label: It appears that 什么意思?

readingSteps.exerciseLabel: Match each section to its function — then prove it from the text.
readingSteps.subtitle: Find the skeleton
readingSteps.summary:
Text structure: History → Culture → Conclusion.

The writer builds an argument step by step. Next, let's collect the evidence.

sections.hint: Look for TIME signals — phrases pointing to a time period: "change over time", "ancient Egypt", "the 1600s", "Elizabethan England".
sections.hintZh: 找时间信号——指向时间段的短语，包括古代地名（如 "ancient Egypt"）。
sections.aiCorrect: Exactly — all TIME signals: "change over time", "different periods of history", "ancient Egypt", "the 1600s", "Elizabethan England". Time words → History.
sections.aiPartial: You're mixing time markers with descriptive details. Which words specifically point to a *time period*?

sections.hint: Look for PLACE signals — country / culture names: "Borneo", "New Zealand", "Myanmar", "Indonesia", plus "different cultures around the world".
sections.hintZh: 找地点/文化信号——国家名称。
sections.aiCorrect: Yes — all PLACE / CULTURE signals: "different cultures around the world", "Borneo", "New Zealand", "Myanmar", "Indonesia", "cultural identity". Multiple places = Culture.
sections.aiPartial: You picked some practices. Those are *examples*, not signals. What word repeats at the start of each paragraph?

sections.hint: Three moves: SUMMARY phrase ("appears that"), list pulling earlier examples together ("whether… or…"), and final CLAIM ("cultural values, not just vanity").
sections.hintZh: 总结词 + 把前面例子收拢的列表 + 最终判断。
sections.aiCorrect: Perfect — a summary phrase ("appears that"), a list pulling earlier examples together ("whether… or…"), and a final claim. No new evidence — just a verdict.
sections.aiPartial: A conclusion has three moves: summary phrase, refer back to earlier examples, final claim. Find each one.

3.why: Pure TIME signal. This is the topic sentence — the cleanest single cue that ¶3-4 is History.
TIME signal repeated — the writer is being explicit. "Periods of history" can ONLY mean History.

3.t: reveal different views of beauty, particularly of women.

3.why: A historical reference — anchors the example to a TIME period, which is what makes this section History.
A specific beauty ideal. The signal isn't WHAT they valued — it's WHEN ("Egyptian").

3.t: as the normal practice, while one of the earliest representations of women in art in Europe is an overweight female. This is the Venus of Hohle Fels and it is more than 35,000 years old.

4.why: A specific historical period — confirms the History pattern. Time anchors so far: change over time → Egyptian → 1600s.

4.t: , artists like Peter Paul Rubens also painted

4.why: A description of the beauty ideal — interesting content, but not a structural signal.

4.t: who were thought to be the most stunning examples of female beauty at that time. In

4.why: Another historical era. Four time-anchors in two paragraphs = unambiguous History.

4.t: , pale skin was still fashionable because it was a sign of wealth: the make-up used to achieve this look was expensive, so only rich people could afford it.

5.why: Topic sentence. "Different cultures" + "around the world" = pure PLACE/CULTURE signal. This is THE cue for ¶5-7.

5.t: , there is a huge difference in what is considered beautiful. Traditional customs, like

5.why: Practices listed — but the structural signal is the place/culture framing, not what they do.

5.t: or other kinds of bodily changes can express

5.why: The deeper cultural meaning — beauty practices express identity, not just aesthetics.

6.why: A place. The writer announced "different cultures around the world" — and now starts listing them.
A vivid metaphor — content, not a structural signal.

6.t: because they are a written record of all the important events and places a man has experienced in his life. For

6.why: Another place. Pattern emerging: Borneo → New Zealand → (Myanmar next). Locations, not eras = Culture, not History.
Cultural meaning — important content but not the structural cue.

6.t: . Western society used to have a very low opinion of tattoos. Today they are considered a popular form of body art among the new generation.

7.t: For Europeans, the tradition of using

7.why: A vivid practice — but the structural cue is "Myanmar", not what they wear.

7.t: to stretch a girl's neck may be shocking, but the

7.why: Third country in three paragraphs. The pattern is now undeniable — places, not eras.

7.t: people consider women with long and thin necks more elegant. In

7.why: Fourth place. Plus the next phrase says "cultural identity" — the writer is being explicit.

7.t: , the custom of sharpening girls' teeth to points might seem very odd while it is perfectly acceptable in other places to straighten children's teeth with braces. Wearing rings in the nose or plastic surgery might be seen as ugly and unattractive by some cultures, but it is commonplace in many others.

8.why: "It appears that" — classic SUMMARY signal. Argumentative texts use this to introduce conclusions.

8.t: through the ages and across different cultures, people have always changed their bodies and faces for a wide variety of reasons: sometimes to help them look more beautiful, and sometimes to enable them to
show social position or display group identity
8.why: Pulls together the cultural meanings from ¶5-7. Conclusions re-summarize, they don't add new info.

8.t: Whether it is wearing make-up or decorating the body with tattoos, rings and piercings
8.why: The "whether… or…" list pulls earlier examples together — only conclusions do this.

8.t: , different cultures view these things with different eyes. Does this mean that we are all beautiful in our own way?

discuss.goal: Student should understand that the writer organizes the text from History → Culture → Conclusion, building an argument step by step rather than just listing random examples.
discuss.openingQ: You matched the sections to their functions. But here's a deeper question: why does the writer put History BEFORE Culture? Could the order be reversed? What would change?
discuss.openingQZh: 作者为什么把"历史"放在"文化"前面？顺序能不能反过来？会有什么变化？
scaffolds: If the writer started with culture...

clusters.label: 顺序无所谓
clusters.description: 学生认为段落顺序是随机的或无所谓的，没有看到结构意图。示例：'顺序反过来也一样' / 'the writer just put them in any order'

clusters.label: 时间先后逻辑
clusters.description: 学生用时间先后解释顺序（历史发生在前所以先写），但没有看到论证层递关系。示例：'因为历史比文化早' / 'history happened first so it comes first'

clusters.label: 层递论证识别
clusters.description: 学生识别出先历史后文化是为了层层递进——先证明美随时间变，再证明美随空间变，合力支撑结论。示例：'先时间再空间，论据更强' / 'each section adds a new layer to the argument'

targetPoints.label: 顺序非随机
targetPoints.description: 学生认识到History在Culture之前不是偶然的，而是有意为之。示例：'这个顺序是故意的' / 'the order is not random, it's on purpose'

targetPoints.label: 层递论证效果
targetPoints.description: 学生解释了先时间后空间的论证效果：先证明美随时间变，再证明美随空间变，层层递进使论证更有力。示例：'先说历史上变了，再说不同地方也不同，加在一起更convincing' / 'history proves change over time, culture proves change across places'

discuss.systemPrompt:
You are a Socratic English reading tutor for a Chinese high school student reading "Ideal Beauty."

LEARNING GOAL: The student should sense that the order History → Culture is intentional, not random — putting history first and culture second makes the argument more convincing or logical. They do NOT need to explicitly say "time dimension vs space dimension" or use the word "cumulative."

RULES:
- NEVER state the answer directly. Guide through questions.
- Ask ONE focused question per turn. Keep it short (2-3 sentences max).
- If the student says something partially correct, acknowledge it, then probe deeper.
- If stuck, try: "What does the History section prove? What does Culture add on top of that?"
- Use simple English for a Chinese high school learner.
- If the student writes in Chinese, respond in English but show you understood.
- If the student demonstrates full understanding, respond with exactly: [GOAL_REACHED] followed by a brief congratulatory summary.
- Be warm, encouraging, conversational.
- IMPORTANT: Give [GOAL_REACHED] when the student shows meaningful partial understanding — they don't need perfect or complete articulation. If they grasp the core idea even roughly, that counts. Err on the side of encouraging the student rather than blocking them.

fallbackMC.question: Why does the writer discuss History (¶3-4) before Culture (¶5-7)?
fallbackMC.questionZh: 作者为什么先讨论历史（¶3-4），再讨论文化（¶5-7）？
options: Because history happened before culture
Because history is more important than culture
To build a stronger argument: beauty changes across time AND place
The order doesn't matter — it's random
fallbackMC.explanation: The writer builds a layered argument. First, History shows beauty changes across TIME (ancient Egypt, 1600s Europe). Then Culture shows beauty differs across SPACE (Borneo, Maori, Myanmar). Together they prove beauty is neither fixed nor universal — it varies in every dimension. This step-by-step structure makes the conclusion much more convincing.
fallbackMC.explanationZh: 作者层层递进：先用历史证明审美随时间变化，再用文化证明审美随地域不同。两者结合，证明审美既不固定也不统一。

discuss.insight: The text structure is deliberate: History (time) + Culture (space) = beauty is not universal. A layered argument is stronger than a list.
discuss.insightZh: 文章结构是刻意的：历史(时间) + 文化(空间) = 审美不是普适的。层层递进比罗列更有说服力。

teacherView.speechLine: Now read only the **first sentence** of each paragraph. Find the **signal words**.
teacherView.focusSubtitle: 首句 + 转折词 → 三段结构

quickActions.label: 只读首句！
quickActions.message: 注意！只读每段的第一句话，不要读全段。找出关键转折词。

quickActions.label: 信号词提示
quickActions.message: 找这些词：change over time, different cultures, around the world, It appears that — 它们告诉你段落的功能。

quickActions.label: 文章结构
quickActions.message: 文章骨架：¶3-4 历史 → ¶5-7 文化 → ¶8 结论。你找到了吗？

quickActions.label: 再给 2 分钟
quickActions.message: 再给大家 2 分钟时间完成当前任务

cueCards.title: Skimming 方法
cueCards.body:
核心公式：首句 + 信号词 = 段落功能

不要逐字读！3 分钟拿到全文骨架 vs 30 分钟逐字读。
提问："What does 'change over time' tell you about ¶3?"

cueCards.title: 三段结构
cueCards.body:
¶3-4: History — 跨时间（Egypt, 1600s Europe）
¶5-7: Culture — 跨空间（Borneo, Maori, Myanmar, Indonesia）
¶8: Conclusion — 总结（show social position / display group identity）

cueCards.title: 过渡到 Step 3
cueCards.body:
确认学生找到三段结构后：
"Good, we now have the skeleton. Let's fill in the flesh — each group focuses on 2-3 paragraphs and builds a matrix."

studentView.title: What is Skimming?
studentView.body:
Skimming is a fast reading technique: don't read word by word — only read the first sentence of each paragraph and look for signal words.

Why does this work? In expository and argumentative texts, the first sentence is usually the topic sentence — it tells you what the paragraph is about.

Look for these signal words:

  • change over time → the paragraph is about history
  • around the world / different cultures → it's about cultural differences
  • It appears that → it's a summary / conclusion

In the next task, use skimming to read the first sentences of ¶3–8 and decide which structural section each paragraph belongs to.
keyPoints: Key formula: first sentence + signal words = paragraph function
3 minutes of skimming beats 30 minutes of word-by-word reading
After spotting signal words, classify paragraphs as History, Culture, or Conclusion
studentView.ttsText: What is Skimming? Skimming is a fast reading technique: don't read word by word — only read the first sentence of each paragraph and look for signal words. Why does this work? In expository and argumentative texts, the first sentence is usually the topic sentence — it tells you what the paragraph is about. Look for these signal words: "change over time" means the paragraph is about history. "Around the world" or "different cultures" means it's about cultural differences. "It appears that" means it's a summary or conclusion. In the next task, use skimming to read the first sentences of paragraphs 3 to 8 and decide which structural section each paragraph belongs to. Remember: key formula — first sentence plus signal words equals paragraph function. 3 minutes of skimming beats 30 minutes of word-by-word reading. After spotting signal words, classify paragraphs as History, Culture, or Conclusion.

readingSteps.description: 分组寻读：A组找历史证据(¶3-4)，B组找文化证据(¶5-7)。合作完成 Place × Practice × Reason 矩阵表。
readingSteps.subtitle: Collect evidence
readingSteps.summary:
You turned paragraphs into organized evidence.

Your matrix shows beauty is about culture, status, and identity.

Next, use this evidence to form your own opinion.

answers.hint: In ¶4, what body type was "**the most stunning**" beauty? Also: why was **pale skin** fashionable?
answers.hintZh: 在 ¶4 中，什么体型是 "**the most stunning**" 的美？**白皮肤**为什么时髦？

answers.hint: In ¶6: "tattoos are like a **diary**..." — a written record of what?
answers.hintZh: 在 ¶6："tattoos are like a **diary**..."——记录了什么？

answers.hint: In ¶6: Maori tattoos "**reflect**" something about a person. What?
answers.hintZh: 在 ¶6：Maori 的 tattoos "**reflect**" 了一个人的什么？

answers.hint: In ¶7, find "**metal rings**" to stretch a girl's neck. Myanmar people consider long necks more ___?
answers.hintZh: 在 ¶7 中找 "**metal rings**"。缅甸人认为长脖子更加___？

answers.hint: In ¶7, second sentence: "the custom of **sharpening** girls' teeth..." — what was the cultural reason?
answers.hintZh: 在 ¶7 第二句："**sharpening** girls' teeth..."——文化原因是什么？

discuss.goal: Student should see that all these beauty practices are not just about appearance — they reflect deeper cultural meanings like identity, status, belonging, and values.
discuss.openingQ: Look at your completed matrix. If someone from another planet read it, would they conclude that humans care about "looking good"? Or is something deeper going on?
discuss.openingQZh: 看看你填好的矩阵。如果外星人读了它，他们会觉得人类只是在乎"好看"吗？还是有更深层的东西？

clusters.label: 外表层面理解
clusters.description: 学生认为这些审美实践只是为了好看或吸引人，停留在外表层面。示例：'they all want to look beautiful' / '这些都是为了让自己更好看'

clusters.label: 单一文化要素
clusters.description: 学生提到了某个文化含义（如身份或地位）但未泛化到所有实践，只停留在单个例子。示例：'Maori tattoo shows status' / '纹身代表地位'

clusters.label: 文化语言概括
clusters.description: 学生认识到审美实践是文化语言，表达身份、地位、归属感和价值观，不只是外表。示例：'beauty is a cultural language' / '美是一种文化表达，代表你是谁'

targetPoints.label: 超越外表
targetPoints.description: 学生提到审美实践不只是为了好看，还有更深的含义。示例：'not just about looking good' / '不只是外表的事'

targetPoints.label: 具体文化含义
targetPoints.description: 学生能举出至少一个具体的文化含义（身份identity、地位status、归属belonging、价值观values）。示例：'Maori tattoo shows your family status' / '纹身代表你的社会地位'

discuss.systemPrompt:
You are a Socratic English reading tutor for a Chinese high school student reading "Ideal Beauty."

LEARNING GOAL: The student should realize that beauty practices have meanings beyond just looking good — like identity, status, or cultural belonging. Giving at least ONE concrete example from the text that shows deeper meaning is sufficient. They do NOT need to generalize to all practices or say "cultural language."

RULES:
- NEVER state the answer directly. Guide through questions.
- Ask ONE focused question per turn. 2-3 sentences max.
- Try questions like: "What does the Borneo tattoo tell others about the person?" or "Is the Maori tā moko about looking beautiful or about something else?"
- If stuck, pick one specific matrix row and ask about the "why" column.
- Use simple English for a Chinese high school learner.
- If Chinese input, respond in English but show understanding.
- If the student demonstrates full understanding, respond with: [GOAL_REACHED] followed by a brief congratulatory summary.
- Warm, encouraging, conversational.
- IMPORTANT: Give [GOAL_REACHED] when the student shows meaningful partial understanding — they don't need perfect or complete articulation. If they grasp the core idea even roughly, that counts. Err on the side of encouraging the student rather than blocking them.

fallbackMC.question: What do all the beauty practices in the matrix have in common?
fallbackMC.questionZh: 矩阵中所有审美实践的共同点是什么？
options: They all make people look more attractive
They are all painful and dangerous
They all reflect cultural meanings like identity, status, and belonging
They are all from ancient times
fallbackMC.explanation: Every beauty practice in the text carries cultural meaning beyond appearance. Borneo tattoos record life events. Maori tā moko shows social position. Elizabethan pale skin signaled wealth. Indonesian teeth-sharpening expressed cultural identity. Beauty is a cultural language — it communicates who you are, not just how you look.
fallbackMC.explanationZh: 文中每种审美实践都承载着超越外表的文化含义。美是一种文化语言——它传达的是你是谁，而不只是你长什么样。

discuss.insight: Beauty is a cultural language: identity, status, belonging. Not just "looking good."
discuss.insightZh: 审美是一种文化语言：身份、地位、归属感。不只是"好看"。

teacherView.speechLine: Good, we now have the **skeleton**. Let's fill in the **flesh** — **Place × Practice × Reason**.

quickActions.label: Myanmar 位置提示
quickActions.message: Myanmar (¶7a): 缅甸的美容实践是长颈族用铜环拉长脖子，象征美丽和身份

quickActions.label: Practice 写法示例
quickActions.message: Practice 列要写具体做法，例如 "slim dark-haired women in paintings" 而不是 "paintings"

quickActions.label: Maori tattoos
quickActions.message: 原文说 Maori tattoos reflect a person's position in society，注意 Practice 写 tattoos 即可

quickActions.label: 再给 2 分钟
quickActions.message: 再给大家 2 分钟时间完成当前任务

cueCards.title: 示范一行
cueCards.body:
用 Ancient Egypt (¶3) 打样：
• "What did Egyptians value?" → slim dark-haired women
• "How do we know?" → shown in paintings as normal practice

让学生模仿这个模式填其他行。

cueCards.title: 易错点
cueCards.body:
¶6 Maori: Practice 写 tattoos，Reason 是 position in society。原文只说 tattoos，不要写 tā moko。

¶7: Myanmar 与 Indonesia 是两行不同地方，常被合并成一行。提醒分开写。

cueCards.title: 过渡到 Step 4
cueCards.body:
回到矩阵整体，指向 Reason 列：
"Look at the Reason column. What do you see? Are these really 'shallow beauty ideals'?"

studentView.title: What is Scanning?
studentView.body:
Scanning is different from skimming — this time you read with a specific question in mind and locate exact details in the text.

Think of looking up a word in a dictionary: you don't read the whole dictionary — you scan with a clear target and stop when you find it. That's scanning.

Next, you'll build a Place × Practice × Reason matrix:

  • Place — which country or culture?
  • Practice — what do they do to pursue beauty?
  • Reason — why do they do it? (What's the cultural meaning?)

I've filled in the demo row (Ancient Egypt) for you. Your job is to scan ¶5–7 and find the information for the remaining 4 places.
keyPoints: Scanning = read with a purpose, eyes on the target
Each row must answer three questions: Where? What? Why?
Note: the text says 'tattoos' — don't write 'tā moko'
¶7 contains two different places (Myanmar and Indonesia) — fill two separate rows
studentView.ttsText: What is Scanning? Scanning is different from skimming — this time you read with a specific question in mind and locate exact details in the text. Think of looking up a word in a dictionary: you don't read the whole dictionary — you scan with a clear target and stop when you find it. That's scanning. Next, you'll build a Place, Practice, Reason matrix. Place — which country or culture? Practice — what do they do to pursue beauty? Reason — why do they do it? What's the cultural meaning? I've filled in the demo row, Ancient Egypt, for you. Your job is to scan paragraphs 5 to 7 and find the information for the remaining 4 places. Remember: scanning means read with a purpose, eyes on the target. Each row must answer three questions: Where? What? Why? Note: the text says "tattoos" — don't write "tā moko". Paragraph 7 contains two different places — Myanmar and Indonesia — fill two separate rows.

readingSteps.description: 利用矩阵证据挑战 ¶2 的 'shallow beauty ideals'，写一段议论段。

aiHints.q: Can I add my own ideas?
aiHints.label: 可以加自己的想法吗?

readingSteps.exerciseLabel: Drag each practice onto the plane, then explain your reasoning.
readingSteps.subtitle: Do you agree?
readingSteps.summary:
You used evidence to support your judgment — a big step in academic reading.

Let's review how you learned today.

answerKey.prompt: Each chip below is one beauty practice from the text. Drag it onto the plane based on **two questions**, then explain *why* you placed it where you did.

discuss.goal: Student should be able to use text evidence to argue that modern media beauty standards are "shallow" because they ignore the rich cultural meanings behind different beauty practices, reducing beauty to a single physical standard.
discuss.openingQ: The writer calls modern media beauty standards "shallow." That's a strong word. Do you think it's fair? What would make a beauty standard "deep" instead of "shallow"?
discuss.openingQZh: 作者说现代媒体审美是"肤浅的(shallow)"。这个词很重。你觉得公平吗？什么样的审美标准算"深刻"而不是"肤浅"？

clusters.label: 表面否定
clusters.description: 学生只是同意或反对'肤浅'这个词，但没有给出文本证据或解释。示例：'我觉得media beauty确实很shallow' / '肤浅是因为他们只看脸'

clusters.label: 单一证据引用
clusters.description: 学生能引用文中一个例子（如某个文化的审美），但没有形成完整的claim-evidence-explanation论证链。示例：'文章说Borneo的人觉得长耳垂漂亮，所以审美不一样' / '不同地方标准不同所以media太narrow'

clusters.label: 论证链构建
clusters.description: 学生能用文本证据构建完整论证：现代媒体美只推崇单一外表标准(slim/fair)，而忽视了不同文化中美的深层含义(身份/地位/价值观)。示例：'shallow是因为media只看一种physical standard但文章里beauty代表了cultural identity' / '深刻的标准应该像文中说的包含文化意义'

targetPoints.label: shallow含义理解
targetPoints.description: 学生理解shallow指现代媒体只推崇单一外表标准，忽视文化深度。示例：'shallow means only one standard' / '肤浅就是只看一种外表'

targetPoints.label: 文本证据引用
targetPoints.description: 学生引用文中至少一个具体例子来支撑论点。示例：'like Borneo people think long earlobes are beautiful, that's different from media' / '文章说Maori纹身代表地位，这比media标准深刻'

targetPoints.label: 论证链意识
targetPoints.description: 学生展现claim→evidence→explanation的论证结构意识。示例：'media is shallow because (claim) the text shows (evidence) different cultures have deeper meanings (explanation)'

discuss.systemPrompt:
You are a Socratic English reading tutor for a Chinese high school student reading "Ideal Beauty."

LEARNING GOAL: The student should express that media beauty standards are "shallow" because they only focus on one physical standard, while the text shows beauty can carry deeper meanings (identity, status, values). Citing ONE piece of text evidence with a basic explanation is sufficient. A full claim→evidence→explanation chain is ideal but NOT required for [GOAL_REACHED].

RULES:
- NEVER state the answer directly. Guide through questions.
- Ask ONE focused question per turn. 2-3 sentences max.
- Push for EVIDENCE: "Which example from your matrix supports that idea?"
- Push for EXPLANATION: "So what does that prove about 'shallow'?"
- If the student only gives opinion, ask for a text fact.
- If the student only gives a fact, ask what it proves.
- Help them build the claim → evidence → explanation chain.
- Use simple English for a Chinese high school learner.
- If Chinese input, respond in English but show understanding.
- If the student demonstrates a complete argument with evidence, respond with: [GOAL_REACHED] followed by a brief congratulatory summary.
- Warm, encouraging, conversational.
- IMPORTANT: Give [GOAL_REACHED] when the student shows meaningful partial understanding — they don't need perfect or complete articulation. If they grasp the core idea even roughly, that counts. Err on the side of encouraging the student rather than blocking them.

fallbackMC.question: Why does the writer call modern media beauty standards "shallow"?
fallbackMC.questionZh: 作者为什么说现代媒体审美标准是"肤浅的"？
options: Because modern beauty is not real
Because media only promotes one physical standard and ignores the cultural meanings of beauty
Because beautiful people are not smart
Because ancient beauty was better than modern beauty
fallbackMC.explanation: The writer uses evidence from many cultures to show that beauty has always carried deep meaning — identity, status, cultural values. Modern media reduces all of this to ONE narrow physical standard (slim, fair). That's why it's "shallow" — it strips away the cultural richness and replaces it with a single look. A strong argument uses this pattern: Position → Evidence → Explanation.
fallbackMC.explanationZh: 作者用多元文化证据表明，审美一直承载着深层含义。现代媒体把这一切简化为单一外表标准，所以是"肤浅的"。

discuss.insight: Claim → Evidence → Explanation. "Shallow" means ignoring cultural depth. Strong arguments need all three links.
discuss.insightZh: 观点→证据→解释。"肤浅"意味着忽视文化深度。好的论证需要三者缺一不可。

teacherView.speechLine: Look at the **Reason column** in your matrix. Are these really **shallow beauty ideals**?
teacherView.focusTitle: 评分标准 & 写作模板
teacherView.focusSubtitle: 议论段：立场 + 矩阵证据

quickActions.label: 回顾 Reason 列
quickActions.message: 回看你的矩阵 Reason 列：wealth, diary, social position, cultural identity — 这些算 shallow 吗？

quickActions.label: 引用矩阵证据
quickActions.message: 写议论段时，至少引用矩阵中 2 个地方的证据。例如：In Borneo... In New Zealand...

quickActions.label: 议论段模板
quickActions.message: 模板：I disagree that beauty ideals are shallow. First, in [Place], [Practice] because [Reason]. Second, in [Place]... Therefore, beauty is not shallow.

quickActions.label: 再给 2 分钟
quickActions.message: 再给大家 2 分钟时间完成当前任务

cueCards.title: 引导批判思维
cueCards.body:
¶2 说 "shallow beauty ideals" — 但这是 many people 的看法，不是作者的结论。

提问："Does the author agree? What evidence does he give?"
学生需要从矩阵的 Reason 列找反驳证据。

cueCards.title: 评分标准
cueCards.body:
三个评分点：
1. 立场清晰（agree / disagree / partly agree）
2. 引用 ≥ 2 条矩阵事实作为证据
3. 用 First / Second / Therefore 组织段落结构

cueCards.title: 过渡到 Step 5
cueCards.body:
选 1-2 篇好的学生作品展示，然后引导：
"Great arguments. Now let's step back — what reading strategies did we use today?"

studentView.title: How to Read Critically
studentView.body:
You've already used skimming to find the skeleton and scanning to extract details. Now comes the most important step — Evaluating.

Go back to ¶2, where the writer says:

"Many people are worried that modern media promotes shallow beauty ideals."

Notice: this is what "many people" think — it's not necessarily the writer's conclusion.

Your job is to use the factual evidence from your matrix to judge: are these beauty practices really "shallow"?

Argumentative paragraph template:

I [agree/disagree] that beauty ideals are shallow.
First, in [Place], [Practice] because [Reason].
Second, in [Place], [Practice] because [Reason].
Therefore, beauty is [not] shallow.
keyPoints: Distinguish opinion from fact: "many people are worried" is an opinion, not a conclusion
Support your position with at least 2 pieces of evidence from your matrix
Organize your paragraph with First / Second / Therefore
There's no single right answer — agree, disagree, or partly agree are all fine, as long as you have evidence
studentView.ttsText: How to Read Critically. You've already used skimming to find the skeleton and scanning to extract details. Now comes the most important step — Evaluating. Go back to paragraph 2, where the writer says: "Many people are worried that modern media promotes shallow beauty ideals." Notice: this is what "many people" think — it's not necessarily the writer's conclusion. Your job is to use the factual evidence from your matrix to judge: are these beauty practices really "shallow"? Here's an argumentative paragraph template: I agree or disagree that beauty ideals are shallow. First, in a certain place, people do a certain practice because of a certain reason. Second, another example. Therefore, beauty is or is not shallow. Remember: distinguish opinion from fact — "many people are worried" is an opinion, not a conclusion. Support your position with at least 2 pieces of evidence from your matrix. Organize your paragraph with First, Second, Therefore. There's no single right answer — agree, disagree, or partly agree are all fine, as long as you have evidence.

readingSteps.description: 回顾 4 步阅读策略：Predict → Skim → Scan+Matrix → Evaluate。布置作业。

aiHints.q: Do these work for other texts?
aiHints.label: 其他文章也能用吗?

readingSteps.exerciseLabel: Put today's 4 reading strategies in the correct order.
readingSteps.subtitle: Review & transfer
readingSteps.summary:
Reading process: Predict → Skim → Scan → Evaluate.

These work for any text. Keep using them!

Homework: "Beyond the Plate" using today's 4 steps.

items: Predicting — read the title, ask questions

discuss.goal: Student should be able to name and explain the 4 reading strategies (Predict, Skim, Scan, Evaluate) in order, and understand that this process is transferable to any argumentative text.
discuss.openingQ: Imagine your friend missed today's class and asks: "How did you manage to read such a long English article?" How would you explain your reading process step by step?
discuss.openingQZh: 假设你朋友缺课了，问你："你怎么读完这么长的英文文章的？"你会怎么一步步解释？
scaffolds: First, I looked at the title and...
Then I read the first sentences to...

clusters.label: 笼统回忆
clusters.description: 学生只能笼统地说'我就一段段读的'或'我先看了标题'，无法区分不同阅读策略的目的。示例：'我就从头读到尾' / '先看标题然后读文章'

clusters.label: 部分策略识别
clusters.description: 学生能说出2-3个策略但遗漏某个步骤，或者能列出步骤但说不清每步的具体目的。示例：'先predict然后scan找细节最后evaluate' / '我先猜了内容然后做了matrix但忘了中间还有skim'

clusters.label: 完整策略表述
clusters.description: 学生能按顺序说出4个策略(Predict→Skim→Scan→Evaluate)及各自目的，并认识到这个过程可以迁移到其他文章。示例：'先predict提出问题，然后skim看结构，再scan找细节建matrix，最后evaluate用证据判断，这个方法可以用在别的文章上'

targetPoints.label: 策略命名
targetPoints.description: 学生能说出至少3个阅读策略的名称（Predict/Skim/Scan/Evaluate）。示例：'we did predict, skim, scan and evaluate' / '我们用了预测、略读、寻读'

targetPoints.label: 策略目的区分
targetPoints.description: 学生能解释不同策略各自的目的，而不只是列出名称。示例：'predict是先猜内容，skim是看结构' / 'scanning helps find specific details for the matrix'

targetPoints.label: 可迁移认知
targetPoints.description: 学生意识到这套策略可以用于其他文章，不只是这一篇。示例：'这个方法可以用在别的文章上' / 'I can use these steps for any article'

discuss.systemPrompt:
You are a Socratic English reading tutor for a Chinese high school student reading "Ideal Beauty."

LEARNING GOAL: The student should recall most of the reading strategies used today (at least 3 of 4: Predict, Skim, Scan, Evaluate) and have some sense of what each one helped them do. Mentioning transferability to other texts is a bonus, NOT a requirement for [GOAL_REACHED].

RULES:
- NEVER state the answer directly. Guide through questions.
- Ask ONE focused question per turn. 2-3 sentences max.
- If they name a step, ask: "What did that step help you do specifically?"
- If they skip a step, ask: "What did you do BEFORE scanning for details?"
- If they list all 4, ask: "Could you use these same steps for a different article? How?"
- Use simple English for a Chinese high school learner.
- If Chinese input, respond in English but show understanding.
- If the student names all 4 strategies with their purposes AND mentions transferability, respond with: [GOAL_REACHED] followed by a brief congratulatory summary.
- Warm, encouraging, conversational.
- IMPORTANT: Give [GOAL_REACHED] when the student shows meaningful partial understanding — they don't need perfect or complete articulation. If they grasp the core idea even roughly, that counts. Err on the side of encouraging the student rather than blocking them.

fallbackMC.question: What is the correct order of the 4 reading strategies used today?
fallbackMC.questionZh: 今天用的4个阅读策略，正确顺序是什么？
options: Scanning → Skimming → Predicting → Evaluating
Evaluating → Predicting → Scanning → Skimming
Predicting → Skimming → Scanning → Evaluating
Skimming → Predicting → Evaluating → Scanning
fallbackMC.explanation: The reading process follows a logical order: 1) **Predicting** — look at the title, form expectations. 2) **Skimming** — read first sentences to find the text structure. 3) **Scanning** — locate specific details and organize them (matrix). 4) **Evaluating** — use evidence to form your own judgment. This process works for any argumentative or expository text!
fallbackMC.explanationZh: 阅读过程遵循逻辑顺序：预测→略读→寻读→评价。这个过程适用于任何议论文或说明文！

discuss.insight: Predict → Skim → Scan → Evaluate. A transferable process for any argumentative text.
discuss.insightZh: 预测→略读→寻读→评价。适用于任何议论文的可迁移阅读方法。

teacherView.speechLine: Let's recap. We used **4 strategies** today: Predicting, Skimming, Scanning, and Evaluating.

quickActions.label: Predict 回顾
quickActions.message: Step 1 我们用了 Predicting：看标题 + ¶1，预测文章讲什么。这帮你准备好了阅读方向。

quickActions.label: Skim 回顾
quickActions.message: Step 2 我们用了 Skimming：只读首句 + 信号词，3 分钟拿到文章骨架。

quickActions.label: 布置作业
quickActions.message: 作业：用今天学的 4 步法阅读 "Beyond the Plate"。提交 structure map + matrix。下节课对比讲评。

quickActions.label: 再给 2 分钟
quickActions.message: 再给大家 2 分钟时间完成当前任务

cueCards.title: 策略总结
cueCards.body:
今天的 4 步阅读法：
1. Predict — 看标题，激活已知
2. Skim — 读首句，抓骨架
3. Scan — 细读填矩阵，提取证据
4. Evaluate — 用证据批判，写议论段

这套方法适用于任何说明文/议论文。

cueCards.title: ¶8 主旨
cueCards.body:
"It appears that ... people have always changed their bodies and faces ... to show social position or display group identity."

核心观点：美不是 shallow vanity，而是 cultural language — 表达身份、归属、地位。

结尾提问："Does this mean that we are all beautiful in our own way?"

cueCards.title: 作业说明
cueCards.body:
课后阅读 "Beyond the Plate"：
1. 用 4 步法自主阅读
2. 画 structure map（文章骨架图）
3. 填 matrix 表
4. 下节课对比讲评

提交到学生端即可。

studentView.title: Review: What Did You Learn Today?
studentView.body:
Congratulations — you've completed all the reading tasks! Let's review the 4 reading strategies you used today:

Step 1Predicting — read the title, activate prior knowledge
Step 2Skimming — read first sentences + signal words, find the skeleton
Step 3Scanning — read with a purpose, extract details into a matrix
Step 4Evaluating — think critically with evidence, write an argument

This method works for any expository or argumentative text. Try the 4-step approach next time you face a reading comprehension test!
keyPoints: The order of the 4 steps matters: Predict → Skim → Scan → Evaluate
The main idea is in ¶8: beauty is not shallow vanity — it's a cultural language
Homework: read "Beyond the Plate" using the same 4-step method
studentView.confirmLabel: Got it — show me the recap
studentView.ttsText: Review: What Did You Learn Today? Congratulations — you've completed all the reading tasks! Let's review the 4 reading strategies you used today. Step 1, Predicting — read the title, activate prior knowledge. Step 2, Skimming — read first sentences plus signal words, find the skeleton. Step 3, Scanning — read with a purpose, extract details into a matrix. Step 4, Evaluating — think critically with evidence, write an argument. This method works for any expository or argumentative text. Try the 4-step approach next time you face a reading comprehension test! Remember: the order of the 4 steps matters — Predict, Skim, Scan, Evaluate. The main idea is in paragraph 8: beauty is not shallow vanity — it's a cultural language. Homework: read "Beyond the Plate" using the same 4-step method.

lesson.title: Ideal Beauty
lesson.subtitle: 高一(3)班 · 阅读策略训练

data.text: Many people are worried that modern media promotes shallow beauty ideals.
作者真的同意 "shallow" 吗？带着这个怀疑读下去。
找证据：每个文化都有自己的"理想"，这算 shallow 吗？
每段只读首句 + 转折词 = 拿到全文骨架。3 分钟 vs 30 分钟。

data.expr: 首句 + [change / within / It appears] = 段落功能
data.caption: 这是 skimming 的核心公式
答题句型 — 用矩阵每一行套这句话

data.author: 林雅婷
data.text: In Borneo, people get tattoos because they record important life events — like a diary on skin.

data.author: 陈思远
data.text: In Myanmar, women wear neck rings because they look beautiful.
思远 → 雅婷：从 "好看" 到 "文化意义"。回看 ¶8 "show social position or display group identity"
作者只是引用了 "many people" 的看法 — 这是一个观点，不是结论。

branches.label: Partly agree
leaves: BUT: 不同文化都有 "理想"

data.text:
评分点：
1. 立场清晰
2. 引用 >= 2 条矩阵事实
3. 用 First / Second / Therefore 组段

data.expr: I [agree/disagree] that ... First, in [Place]... Second, in [Place]... Therefore, beauty is [not] shallow.
data.caption: 议论段模板

data.text: It appears that ... people have always changed their bodies and faces ... to show social position or display group identity.
HW：用今天的 4 步法读 "Beyond the Plate"。提交 structure map + matrix。下节课对比讲评。

observationIndicators.label: 冲突识别与预测
observationIndicators.description: 能识别¶1和¶2的对立观点（尼日利亚增肥=美 vs 媒体推崇纤瘦），并理解作者刻意用这种对比开头（conflict opening）来激发读者思考的写作意图

observationIndicators.label: 信号词与文章结构
observationIndicators.description: 通过首句信号词识别三段结构：History→Culture→Conclusion。区分时间词(change over time)和地点词(different cultures)

observationIndicators.label: 矩阵信息定向提取
observationIndicators.description: 用 scanning 从¶3-7定向提取Place×Practice×Reason三维信息，准确对应文化与证据

observationIndicators.label: 审美深层规律洞察
observationIndicators.description: 从矩阵的Reason列归纳出共性：审美不只是外表，而是文化身份、社会地位、财富的表达

observationIndicators.label: 证据支撑的批判立场
observationIndicators.description: 对¶2 shallow beauty ideals形成有理有据的立场，引用至少2条矩阵证据，区分事实与观点

observationIndicators.label: 四步策略元认知
observationIndicators.description: 理解Predict→Skim→Scan→Evaluate的顺序和每步的目的，能迁移到新文章

observationIndicators.label: 字面理解局限
observationIndicators.description: 只理解表面意思：如把fattening room当健康问题、把standard of beauty译为「标准美」、不理解beauty is only skin deep的比喻义

observationIndicators.label: 信号词辨识困难
observationIndicators.description: 无法区分时间信号词和地点信号词，把History和Culture段落搞混，或把¶8当成文化段而非结论

observationIndicators.label: 跨文化细节张冠李戴
observationIndicators.description: 把A文化的做法/原因归给B文化：如Borneo纹身说成Maori的，Myanmar颈环说成Indonesia的

observationIndicators.label: 观点事实不分
observationIndicators.description: 把¶2 many people are worried当作作者结论或全文事实，不理解这是引述他人观点；或自己论证时不区分观点和事实

observationIndicators.label: 无证据空洞论述
observationIndicators.description: 表达立场时只给个人感受不引用文本证据，或论证逻辑断裂（有立场无论据，有论据无推理）

observationIndicators.label: 阅读策略混用
observationIndicators.description: 混淆skimming和scanning（如scanning时逐字精读），或不理解策略顺序的意义（如跳过predict直接读细节）

bonusArticle.title: Beyond the Plate: Decoding Table Manners

paragraphs.text: Every culture has unwritten rules about how food should be eaten. These rules — table manners — may seem trivial, but they carry deep meaning about respect, community, and identity. What counts as polite in one country can be rude in another.

In Japan, slurping noodles loudly is not just acceptable — it is a compliment to the chef. The sound signals that the diner is enjoying the meal. Silence at the table, by contrast, might suggest the food is bland or unappreciated.

In parts of India and the Middle East, eating with your hands is the traditional norm. Using the right hand to pick up rice or bread connects the eater physically to the food, and many people believe this enhances the sensory experience of dining.

Western etiquette often expects diners to finish everything on their plate — leaving food behind may be seen as wasteful or impolite to the host. Yet in parts of China, leaving a little food signals that the host has been generous enough to provide more than enough.

Table manners, like beauty standards, reveal that no single set of customs is universally "correct." Understanding different dining traditions helps us appreciate cultural diversity and avoid judging others by our own standards alone.

bonusSteps.label: 结构识别
bonusSteps.exerciseLabel: Match each paragraph to its structural function.

options: Introduction
Example: sounds
Example: hands
Example: plates
Conclusion

bonusSteps.label: 矩阵构建
bonusSteps.exerciseLabel: Complete the culture × custom × meaning matrix.

answers.reason: leaving food is seen as wasteful

answers.practice: leaving a little food on the plate
